abstract: Although the oxidative addition of Ni(I) to aryl iodides is a frequently invoked step in catalytic cycles, a comprehensive mechanistic foundation for this fundamental transformation remains elusive. In this work, we present an in-depth mechanistic investigation of this process employing a combination of electroanalytical methods and statistical modeling. Through the use of electrochemistry, we rapidly quantified oxidative addition rates across a broad library of aryl iodide substrates and four structurally distinct, catalytically relevant ligand classes (Ni(MeBPy), Ni(MePhen), Ni(Terpy), and Ni(BPP)). Leveraging a dataset of more than 200 experimental rate measurements, we utilized multivariate linear regression to isolate the key electronic and steric parameters governing reactivity. Our findings reveal a mechanistic bifurcation where the pathway—either a concerted three-center transition state or a halogen-atom abstraction—is dictated primarily by the ligand architecture. Finally, we developed a global heatmap of predicted oxidative addition rates, demonstrating its utility in rationalizing reaction outcomes through a case study of a Ni-catalyzed cross-coupling transformation.
